Title: The importance of maltose in transitory starch breakdown

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal carbon export intermediate from chloroplasts; implicated in stress adaptation through maltose homeostasis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf chloroplast envelope/cytosol interface; leaves at night
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for chloroplast maltose export during transitory starch breakdown; loss causes strong maltose accumulation, reduced starch degradation, starch excess, and growth retardation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MEX1 is the plastid maltose transporter on the chloroplast envelope. 2. MEX1-deficient plants accumulate up to ~100-fold more maltose and show reduced starch degradation and growth retardation. 3. MEX1 and DPE1 define parallel export-linked routes with glucose export, as mex1/dpe1 double mutants are more severe than either parent.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; non-aqueous fractionation; metabolite profiling; double-mutant genetic interaction
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function causes growth retardation and starch-excess phenotype; severe synthetic defects with dpe1.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: β-maltose appears to be the metabolically active anomer, and export may occur passively down a concentration gradient.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for engineering nocturnal carbon export and starch remobilization, but reduced function strongly impairs growth.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal carbon export intermediate converted in the cytosol toward sucrose synthesis
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for cytosolic maltose metabolism after chloroplast export; loss causes strong maltose accumulation and reduced starch degradation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPE2 is the first cytosolic enzyme identified for maltose metabolism in Arabidopsis. 2. Plants lacking DPE2 accumulate ~100-fold normal maltose and show reduced starch degradation. 3. DPE2 likely acts on maltose together with a branched cytosolic heteroglycan, analogous to bacterial MalQ-mediated maltose metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; native glycogen gel activity assays; recombinant protein assays; phylogenetic comparison
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss impairs starch remobilization and may contribute to growth defects under some conditions.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cytosolic disproportionating enzyme / 4-α-glucanotransferase with predicted C-terminal polysaccharide-binding domain
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DPE2 protein abundance may oscillate diurnally in potato leaves; highest activity reported with glycogen or heteroglycan together with maltose.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Night; photoperiod; cold stress discussed as potentially affecting DPE2 activity indirectly
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression/protein amount can show diurnal behavior in potato; in Arabidopsis maltose-metabolizing genes are clock regulated overall
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising target for engineering nocturnal carbon conversion to sucrose, but perturbation strongly elevates maltose and impairs starch turnover.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Part of nocturnal carbon remobilization from maltose/heteroglycan toward sucrose precursor pools
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely contributes to cytosolic metabolism of maltose-derived heteroglycan; deficiency is associated with elevated night maltose
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Pho2 is proposed to function in cytosolic heteroglycan metabolism analogous to bacterial maltodextrin phosphorylase. 2. Pho2 uses soluble heteroglycan as glucosyl donor or acceptor depending on Pi/G1P ratio. 3. A Pho2-deficient Arabidopsis mutant showed relatively higher maltose at night, supporting involvement in cytosolic maltose metabolism.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical interaction assays with soluble heteroglycan; antisense/RNAi studies in potato; mutant metabolite observation in Arabidopsis
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Not clearly established; antisense inhibition in potato showed little overall carbohydrate phenotype.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Reaction direction is inferred to favor phosphorolysis to G1P in vivo at night because cytosolic Pi greatly exceeds G1P.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential secondary target to improve cytosolic conversion of maltose-derived carbon, but evidence is less definitive than for MEX1/DPE2.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Hydrolytic branch of chloroplast starch breakdown leading to maltose export
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cleaves maltosyl units from the non-reducing ends of α(1-4)-linked glucan chains to release maltose
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal carbon export intermediate from chloroplasts; may contribute to freezing protection under cold stress
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf chloroplast/cytosol during transitory starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes maltose production and supports transitory starch degradation; reduced BAM3 function is associated with starch excess and reduced cold-induced maltose accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAM3 encodes a functional plastidial β-amylase implicated as a key enzyme for transitory starch breakdown. 2. BAM3 expression shows strong circadian and photoperiod-dependent regulation, peaking during the day in long days and at night in short days. 3. BAM3 is induced by cold treatment, but maltose accumulation can precede BAM3 transcript induction under cold shock.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control enzyme for starch breakdown rate adjustment to day length; strict rate-limiting status not definitively established
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Clock regulated; transcript peaks during day in long days and at night in short days; induced by cold
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaf expression with pronounced circadian rhythm; cold inducible
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Photoperiod/day length; continuous light; continuous darkness; cold stress at 4–5°C; photorespiratory conditions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi/down-regulation lines; transcript profiling across photoperiods; metabolite profiling under cold stress and continuous light
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review notes that protein abundance may remain relatively stable despite oscillating transcripts, implying post-transcriptional/post-translational regulation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target to tune nocturnal starch mobilization and cold-responsive maltose production, but manipulation should consider photoperiod sensitivity and possible uncoupling between transcript and protein levels.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced function is associated with starch excess; broader growth/yield impacts not fully established in this review.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Glucose and longer maltooligosaccharides such as maltopentaose
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers maltosyl units between α-1,4-glucans; in the relevant reaction converts maltotriose to glucose plus a longer glucan
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supports formation of exportable sugars by remodeling maltooligosaccharides during starch breakdown
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal carbon export intermediate from chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for efficient transitory starch degradation; loss causes maltotriose accumulation and starch-excess phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPE1 is a functional plastid-targeted D-enzyme important for transitory starch degradation. 2. dpe1 mutants accumulate maltodextrins, especially maltotriose, and show reduced starch degradation. 3. DPE1 likely has a conserved role in higher plants based on phylogenetic relatedness among Arabidopsis, rice, and potato proteins.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important pathway step, but not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotype analysis; recombinant enzyme substrate assays; phylogenetic comparison
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: mex1/dpe1 double mutants show more severe growth retardation than either parent, indicating parallel chloroplast export routes for maltose and glucose.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target for modulating nocturnal carbon remobilization, though disruption causes starch-excess and growth defects.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss leads to starch excess and severe growth retardation in combination with mex1.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Starch granule phosphorylation step initiating degradation
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Amylopectin/starch granule glucosyl residues and ATP
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers the β-phosphate of ATP to the C6 or C3 positions of glucosyl residues in amylopectin, phosphorylating starch granules
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Enables access to starch and thereby supplies precursor flux toward maltose production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal carbon export intermediate from chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for efficient transitory starch degradation; reduced activity decreases starch phosphate and causes starch-excess phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GWD1 phosphorylates starch granules and is essential for transitory starch degradation. 2. Reduction or loss of GWD1 activity lowers starch phosphate content and causes starch excess. 3. GWD activity is redox regulated, with reduced thioredoxins activating the oxidized enzyme.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Established as an essential initiating/control step for starch granule degradation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Protein level relatively stable across day/night; activity redox regulated
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Night-time starch degradation; redox state; dark adaptation; photorespiratory/starch-degrading conditions
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Antisense and mutant analysis; purified protein assays; radiolabel/phosphate transfer assays; redox activation assays
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Potato GWD is activated by reduced thioredoxin f and m; authors speculate phosphorylated granules synthesized by GWD are properly degraded at night.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for engineering starch turnover, but reduced activity causes starch-excess phenotypes and altered carbon partitioning.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Impaired function causes starch excess and may compromise nocturnal carbon export.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Secondary starch phosphorylation step on prephosphorylated granules
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Further phosphorylated starch glucans, primarily at C3
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Autophosphorylates on a His residue and transfers the β-phosphate of ATP primarily to the C3 position of glucosyl residues on GWD-prephosphorylated starch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Enables/accessorizes starch granule degradation and thus precursor release toward maltose formation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major nocturnal carbon export intermediate from chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Important for transitory starch degradation; repression causes starch-excess phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PWD is a GWD-related enzyme that preferentially acts on starch prephosphorylated by GWD. 2. PWD primarily phosphorylates the C3 position of glucosyl residues. 3. Repression of PWD leads to starch excess, supporting an important role in transitory starch degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important control step in granule priming/modification; not explicitly called rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein phosphorylation assays; mutant/repression phenotype analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Requires prior GWD-mediated phosphorylation for preferred activity.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter starch turnover efficiency, but repression produces starch-excess phenotypes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced activity leads to starch excess.
